papers.title: An observational study of rotation and binarity of Galactic O-type runaway stars
papers.author_names: M. Carretero-Castrillo M. Ribó J. M. Paredes G. Holgado C. Martínez-Sebastián S. Simón-Díaz

abstract.text: Gaia DR3 data have revealed new massive runaway stars, while spectroscopic surveys enable detailed characterization. The relative contributions of binary supernova (BSS) and dynamical ejection (DES) scenarios to explain their runaway origin remain poorly constrained, particularly in the Milky Way. We aim to characterize the largest sample of Galactic O-type runaway stars ever investigated through their kinematics, rotation, and binarity to shed light into their origins. We use the GOSC-Gaia DR3 catalog, and IACOB spectroscopic information to build a sample with 214 O-type stars with projected rotational velocities ($v \\\\sin{i}$), and a subsample of 168 O-type stars with additional information about their likely single (LS) or single-lined (SB1) spectroscopic binary nature. We also consider an additional sample of 65 double-lined (SB2) spectroscopic binaries. We find that among our sample of Galactic O-type runaways, most (74%) have $v \\\\sin{i}<200$ km/s, whereas for normal stars this fraction is slightly higher (82%). There are no fast-moving runaways being fast rotators, except for HD 124 979. Runaways show lower SB1 fractions than normal stars, with no runaway SB1 fast-rotating systems; on average, runaways rotate faster than normal stars; and their runaway fraction is higher among fast rotators (44%) vs. the slow rotators (34%). This is consistent with BSS dominance for fast rotators. We also found that SB2 systems hardly reach runaway velocities with a low runaway fraction (10%). Runaways with 2D velocities > 60 km/s are mostly single and interpreted as DES products, while runaways with 2D velocities > 85 km/s are also interpreted as two-step products. Three of 12 runaway SB1 systems are HMXBs. Our study reveals that most Galactic O-type runaways are slow rotators, suggests a dominance of BSS among fast-rotating runaways, and of DES and two-step among the high-velocity ones. (Abridged)

brief.tldr: [observational study] This study analyzes 214 Galactic O-type runaway stars using Gaia DR3 kinematics and IACOB spectroscopy to assess rotational velocities ($v\sin{i}$) and binarity. It finds that runaways are predominantly slow rotators (74% with $v\sin{i} < 200$ km s⁻¹), with no fast-rotating runaway SB1 systems, and shows a clear dominance of binary supernova (BSS) scenarios among fast rotators, while high-velocity runaways (>85 km s⁻¹) are linked to two-step ejection processes involving both DES and BSS. SB2 binaries rarely achieve runaway velocities, and three runaway SB1 systems are identified as high-mass X-ray binaries, suggesting a pathway to compact-object binaries.

source_fields.title: An observational study of rotation and binarity of Galactic O-type runaway stars

source_fields.sections_text: Data: This section presents a catalog of 106 Galactic O-type runaway stars identified from Gaia DR3 data using a self-consistent $E$ parameter method, supplemented by spectroscopic properties such as projected rotational velocities and binarity signatures from the IACOB project. Results: The experiment finds that 64% of normal O-type stars and 36% of runaway stars are slow rotators, with only a small fraction (5 runaway stars and 1 outlier) exhibiting fast rotation, and the fast-moving and fast-rotating region contains just one runaway star (HD 124 979), indicating a strong correlation between slow rotation and typical runaway dynamics. Discussion: The majority of Galactic O-type runaways and nearly all walkaways are slow rotators, with no fast-moving and fast-rotating runaways, and SB1 systems are rare among runaways, indicating that dynamical ejection mechanisms differ between the Galactic field and the LMC's 30 Dor region. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel analysis of runaway O-type stars by combining dynamical ejection and binary supernova scenarios to resolve the uncertainty in their origin, addressing the problem of how massive runaway stars form and evolve in the Milky Way. Summary and conclusions: This study reveals that Galactic O-type runaway stars are predominantly slow rotators, with binaries less likely to be runaways, supporting dynamical ejection scenarios (DES) and suggesting a significant role of binary disruption, while fast rotators and high-velocity runaways point to binary stellar system (BSS) contributions. Appendix A Data of the rotation sample: This section presents detailed observational data for the first 10 stars in the rotation sample with the highest energy ($E$) values, including their positions, velocities, rotational parameters, and spectral classifications. Appendix B 2D space velocity distributions: The 2D space velocity distributions of fast and slow rotators show no statistically significant differences across rotation, runaway, and normal stars, with the exception of limited statistics in fast-rotating runaways. Appendix C Cumulative distributions considering information on binarity: The CDFs of $v\\sin{i}$ show no statistically significant differences between normal and runaway stars in either LS or SB1 categories, with only a few comparisons approaching the 5% significance threshold.

abstract: Gaia DR3 data have revealed new massive runaway stars, while spectroscopic surveys enable detailed characterization.
We aim to characterize the largest sample of Galactic O-type runaway stars ever investigated through their kinematics, rotation, and binarity to shed light into their origins.

We use the GOSC-Gaia DR3 catalog, and IACOB spectroscopic information to build a sample with 214 O-type stars with projected rotational velocities ($v \\\\sin{i}$), and a subsample of 168 O-type stars
We find that among our sample of Galactic O-type runaways, most (74%) have $v \\\\sin{i}<200$ km/s, whereas for normal stars this fraction is slightly higher (82%).

Runaways show lower SB1 fractions than normal stars, with no runaway SB1 fast-rotating systems; on average, runaways rotate faster than normal stars; and their runaway fraction is higher among fast rotators
title: An observational study of rotation and binarity of Galactic O-type runaway stars

papers.title: Threading the Magellanic Needle: Hypervelocity Stars Trace the Past Location of the LMC

abstract.text: Recent discoveries have shown that a population of hypervelocity stars (HVSs) originate from the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC). We use three such HVSs as dynamical tracers to constrain the past orbit of the LMC. Since each star was ejected at a finite time in the past, it must intersect the past position of the LMC's central black hole at its ejection time. We model the LMC's orbit under the influence of dynamical friction and extended mass distributions for both the LMC and the Milky Way, generating a large ensemble of orbital realizations. By evaluating which orbits intersect the back-integrated HVS trajectories, we compute posterior distributions over the LMC's orbital history. This approach provides significantly tighter constraints on the past motion of the LMC than previously possible. We find two previously published orbital models that are consistent with these new constraints: a first-passage trajectory from a self-consistent hydrodynamic simulation, and a second-passage trajectory from a collisionless N-body simulation. In parallel, we infer the present-day ejection site of the HVSs -- likely tracing the LMC's dynamical center and supermassive black hole -- independent of conventional methods.

brief.tldr: [research paper] This paper uses three hypervelocity stars (HVSs) originating from the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) as dynamical tracers to constrain the LMC’s past orbital position by back-integrating their trajectories and evaluating intersections with modeled LMC orbits under dynamical friction and extended mass distributions. The method yields tighter constraints on the LMC’s historical motion than traditional present-day orbit extrapolations, identifying two previously published orbital models—first- and second-passage—as consistent with data, and independently infers the present-day location of the LMC’s supermassive black hole via joint ejection site analysis.

source_fields.title: Threading the Magellanic Needle: Hypervelocity Stars Trace the Past Location of the LMC

source_fields.sections_text: Methods: The paper proposes a Bayesian orbit integration method that samples LMC initial conditions and integrates hypervelocity star orbits in the LMC–Milky Way potential, evaluating the likelihood of LMC origins based on orbital proximity to the LMC center and combining it with priors to compute posterior probabilities. Results: The study constrains the LMC’s orbital history over the past ∼800 Myr using HVS trajectories, yielding a ∼2× tighter constraint than previous dynamical center-based methods, with the Vasiliev (2024) N-body and Lucchini et al. (2021) hydrodynamical models best matching the HVS-based constraints. Conclusion: This study uses hypervelocity stars to constrain the LMC's past orbit and locate its dynamical center, demonstrating that a Bayesian analysis of just three HVSs significantly narrows orbital possibilities and points to a central black hole at (80.72°, -67.79°), with future improvements in proper motion measurements offering a feasible path to refine these constraints. Discussion: The LMC’s orbital history is best described by a first-passage model with hydrodynamics (Lucchini et al. [2021]), as second-passage scenarios—despite earlier pericentric passages—fail to produce measurable dynamical differences from first-passage models and are inconsistent with present-day observables, highlighting the critical role of hydrodynamic friction in shaping the LMC’s trajectory. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel method using LMC-origin hypervelocity stars to directly constrain the past position of the LMC, providing the first dynamical tracer to distinguish between first- and second-passage scenarios and infer the present location of the LMC’s supermassive black hole. Appendix B Choice of Prior: This paper addresses the problem of determining the dynamical center of the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) under different prior assumptions, contributing to the ongoing debate on the LMC's center location by rigorously testing the sensitivity of results to prior choices in galactic dynamics. Appendix A MW and LMC Masses: Varying the masses of the Milky Way and LMC leads to minor changes in HVS 7's posterior distribution but significant differences in the LMC's orbital morphology, highlighting that galaxy masses remain a key uncertainty in reconstructing the LMC and SMC's orbital history.

abstract: Recent discoveries have shown that a population of hypervelocity stars (HVSs) originate from the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC).
title: Threading the Magellanic Needle: Hypervelocity Stars Trace the Past Location of the LMC
